clean_story:
Stretched days. Warped time.

The phrase "endless summer" isn’t about the season’s length—it’s about how a child’s brain measures time. A seven-year-old’s year is 1/7 of their life; for a 40-year-old, it’s 1/40. Like filling a tiny cup versus a bathtub—the same amount of water (a summer) feels vast in the smaller vessel.

Memories help too. Firsts dominate childhood: first bike ride, first firefly catch, first sunburn. Novelty carves deeper grooves in the brain, making each day feel packed with landmarks. Adult summers blur because routines (commutes, emails) repeat like identical tiles. But a kid’s summer? It’s a collage of scraped knees, popsicle flavors, and the hum of cicadas at dusk—each moment distinct, unrushed by clocks.

Three related subjects are time perception science, how memory shapes aging, and why boredom feels slower.
